network.overall_strategy: Arginine is one of the hardest amino acids for prebiotic synthesis because its guanidinium group cannot be generated by standard Strecker or Miller-Urey chemistry (Miller & Orgel, 1974). The network implements three literature-supported strategies: (1) Sutherland's cyanosulfidic protometabolism (Patel et al., 2015), which builds the full arginine carbon-nitrogen skeleton from HCN, acetylene, NH₃, H₂S, and cyanamide via β-aminopropionaldehyde and compound 46; (2) direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899), with ornithine sourced from spark discharge (Johnson et al., 2008) or from glutamic semialdehyde reductive amination; and (3) the trace Fujioka et al. (2009) supercritical route. Multiple routes to each hub molecule (HCN, cyanamide, ornithine, β-aminopropionaldehyde, pyruvate) create a densely interconnected network spanning hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical environments. Montmorillonite clay concentrates arginine 30-50× by cation exchange (Catalano et al., 2024), potentially overcoming yield limitations.

reactions.name: CO2 reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is adsorbed on the Fe3S4 surface and reduced by H2 via surface-bound hydride transfer, analogous to the biological CO dehydrogenase/acetyl-CoA synthase pathway. Greigite's [4Fe-4S] cubane units activate CO2 by electron donation.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is supported by Preiner et al. (Life, 2020) and by Jordan et al. (Nature Communications, 2021), who showed that greigite's inverse thiospinel structure with [4Fe-4S] units is structurally analogous to biological ferredoxin clusters and can catalyze CO2 fixation under hydrothermal conditions.

reactions.name: Formate amination to formamide

conditions.other: Alkaline vent interior, concentrated NH3 and formate

reactions.mechanism: Ammonolysis of formate to formamide via nucleophilic addition-elimination on mineral surface. Formate is activated by coordination to Fe centers on magnetite, facilitating NH3 attack.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a well-established prebiotic molecule formed from formate and ammonia under hydrothermal conditions. Saladino et al. (Chemistry & Biodiversity, 2007) discussed formamide as a key prebiotic precursor. The reaction is thermodynamically driven at elevated temperatures in concentrated hydrothermal fluids.

reactions.name: Thermal dehydration of formamide to HCN

conditions.other: Mineral surface dehydration at vent margins, gas phase

reactions.mechanism: Thermal dehydration of formamide (HCONH2) on pyrite surface yields HCN + H2O. The mineral surface lowers the activation barrier for dehydration and provides a gas-solid interface.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide thermal decomposition to HCN is well established (Nguyen et al., Journal of Physical Chemistry A, 2011). Pyrite surfaces catalyze dehydration reactions (Cleaves et al., Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, 2010). This connects hydrothermal formate chemistry to the HCN pool needed for cyanosulfidic and Strecker pathways.

reactions.name: Photochemical cyanamide synthesis from HCN and NH3

conditions.other: UV irradiation (<300 nm), shallow pools, anoxic atmosphere

reactions.mechanism: UV photolysis drives radical recombination of HCN-derived CN and NH2 radicals, or photocatalytic oxidative coupling on TiO2 surface, yielding cyanamide (H2N-C≡N). TiO2 generates electron-hole pairs under UV that activate HCN and NH3.
reactions.reasoning: Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin (Science, 1965) demonstrated cyanamide formation from UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures (CH4/NH3/H2O). Cyanamide is the formal adduct of HCN + NH3 minus H2. TiO2 photocatalysis enhances yields (Negrón-Mendoza et al., Amino Acids, 2012). Cyanamide is also a central node in Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) cyanosulfidic network.

reactions.name: Cu(I)-catalyzed cross-coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile

conditions.other: UV irradiation, cyanosulfidic conditions, anaerobic, phosphate buffer

reactions.mechanism: Cu(I) activates acetylene via pi-coordination, facilitating nucleophilic addition of cyanide. Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling is sustained by H2S as reductant. Product acrylonitrile (CH2=CH-CN) is released by beta-elimination.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 7:301-307, 2015) demonstrated this Cu(I)-catalyzed cross-coupling as the first step in the cyanosulfidic pathway to arginine precursors. The reaction proceeds under mild aqueous conditions (35°C) with UV light and H2S, using sulfide mineral surfaces as Cu(I) source.

reactions.name: Michael addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Conjugate (Michael) addition of NH3 to the electron-deficient beta-carbon of acrylonitrile. The clay surface concentrates both reactants and provides mild Lewis acid catalysis via interlayer cations.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) described this Michael addition as part of the cyanosulfidic arginine pathway. Beta-aminopropionitrile (H2N-CH2-CH2-CN) is the product. Michael additions of amines to nitriles are well-known in aqueous organic chemistry and proceed spontaneously at mild pH.

reactions.name: Photoreductive partial reduction of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with H2S as reductant

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox-mediated partial reduction of the nitrile group (-C≡N) to an aldehyde (-CHO) via an imine intermediate. H2S serves as the terminal electron donor, regenerating Cu(I) from Cu(II). The reaction stops at the aldehyde oxidation state rather than proceeding to the amine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) demonstrated photoreductive chemistry in the cyanosulfidic network converting nitriles to aldehydes. Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (Chemical Communications, 53:11893, 2017) confirmed and extended this pathway. Beta-aminopropionaldehyde is the key intermediate that branches to both arginine and proline precursors.

reactions.name: Guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of the primary amino group of beta-aminopropionaldehyde to the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide (H2N-C≡N), forming the guanidine linkage: R-NH2 + H2N-C≡N → R-NH-C(=NH)-NH2. The aldehyde group is preserved for subsequent Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) described this guanylation as 'especially efficient,' producing 3-guanidinopropanal. Cyanamide's electrophilic carbon is highly reactive toward primary amines. This is the critical step that installs the guanidinium group characteristic of arginine. Montmorillonite concentrates cationic intermediates (Catalano et al., Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, 2024).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis on guanylated aldehyde to arginine alpha-aminonitrile (compound 46)
reactions.mechanism: Classic Strecker synthesis: the aldehyde group of 3-guanidinopropanal reacts with NH3 to form an imine (Schiff base), which then undergoes nucleophilic addition of HCN (as cyanide) to yield the alpha-aminonitrile. This produces compound 46, the direct nitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (Nature Chemistry, 2015) explicitly described compound 46 as 'the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine,' produced alongside compound 47 (proline precursor). Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (Chemical Communications, 2017) confirmed this pathway. The Strecker reaction is the most well-established prebiotic route to alpha-amino acids.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile to arginine

conditions.other: Aqueous solution, mildly alkaline pH 8-10, prolonged incubation (days to weeks)

reactions.mechanism: Sequential hydrolysis of the nitrile group: -C≡N + H2O → -C(=O)NH2 (amide intermediate) + H2O → -COOH (carboxylic acid) + NH3. The guanidinium group is stable to hydrolysis (half-life 10^5-10^8 years at pH 9, Robertson & Miller, 1995).
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is the terminal step of all Strecker-based amino acid syntheses. The hydrolysis proceeds through an amide intermediate and is accelerated at elevated temperature and alkaline pH. Robertson & Miller (Journal of Molecular Evolution, 1995) demonstrated that the guanidinium group survives hydrolysis conditions, ensuring arginine rather than ornithine is the product.

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to pyruvate on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction on magnetite/Fe0 surfaces. CO2 is first reduced to formate, then further reduced and condensed through formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde intermediates to pyruvate. Fe0 provides the thermodynamic driving force as a strong reductant.
reactions.reasoning: Cody et al. (Science, 289:1337-1340, 2000) demonstrated pyruvate synthesis from CO under hydrothermal conditions with FeS. Muchowska, Varma & Moran (Nature, 569:104-107, 2019) showed that Fe0 promotes multiple steps of a proto-metabolic network including pyruvate formation from simple C1-C2 precursors. This is the entry point to central carbon metabolism.

reactions.name: Pyruvate to alpha-ketoglutarate via partial reductive TCA steps on Fe0

conditions.other: Alkaline conditions, pH 9-11, sequential steps: pyruvate → oxaloacetate (carboxylation) → malate (reduction) → fumarate (dehydration) → succinate → alpha-ketoglutarate

reactions.mechanism: A sequence of reductive tricarboxylic acid (rTCA) cycle reactions promoted by Fe0: (1) pyruvate + CO2 → oxaloacetate (reductive carboxylation), (2) oxaloacetate → malate (reduction), (3) malate → fumarate → succinate (dehydration + reduction), (4) succinate + CO2 → alpha-ketoglutarate (oxidative decarboxylation analog or reductive carboxylation). Each step is independently promoted by Fe0/magnetite.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma & Moran (Nature, 569:104-107, 2019) demonstrated that Fe0 promotes 6 of 11 rTCA cycle reactions in a single aqueous solution at 70°C (343K), including the formation of succinate and alpha-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and CO2. Varma et al. (PNAS, 115:E10819, 2018) showed similar results. While yields vary for each step, the overall pathway from pyruvate to alpha-ketoglutarate is experimentally substantiated on Fe0 under mild hydrothermal conditions. This is modeled as a multi-step process, not a single reaction.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamic acid

conditions.other: pH 7-9, NH3 in solution, analogous to pyruvate reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid undergoes reductive amination: alpha-ketoglutarate + NH3 → glutamate imine intermediate, which is then reduced by Fe2+ on the mineral surface. The mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide provides both the Lewis acid activation and the reducing equivalents, producing magnetite as a byproduct.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. (PNAS, 116:4828-4833, 2019) demonstrated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxides (1:1 Fe2+:Fe3+, ≥50°C). The same mechanism applies to alpha-ketoglutarate, yielding glutamic acid. Glutamic acid is one of the most abundant amino acids in Miller-Urey experiments and meteorites (Cronin & Chang, 1993; Naraoka et al., Science, 2023), confirming its prebiotic availability.

reactions.name: Reduction of glutamic acid gamma-carboxyl to glutamic semialdehyde

conditions.other: Reducing conditions with Fe2+ or H2S as electron donors, UV-assisted photoreduction possible

reactions.mechanism: Selective reduction of the gamma-carboxyl group of glutamic acid to an aldehyde, producing glutamic-5-semialdehyde (2-amino-5-oxopentanoic acid). This is a two-electron reduction analogous to carboxylate-to-aldehyde reductions observed on iron mineral surfaces. The alpha-amino and alpha-carboxyl groups remain intact.
reactions.reasoning: This is the prebiotically simplest route from glutamate to the ornithine backbone. In biology, gamma-glutamyl kinase + gamma-glutamyl phosphate reductase perform this transformation. Prebiotically, direct carboxyl-to-aldehyde reductions are plausible on Fe mineral surfaces under reducing conditions (Preiner et al., Life, 2020). Zhang et al. (Nature Communications, 2024) showed photocatalytic reductive transformations of amino acids on natural pyrite. This step is partially speculative but mechanistically simple (single functional group reduction) compared to the 8-enzyme biological ornithine pathway.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glutamic semialdehyde to ornithine

conditions.other: Reducing conditions with Fe2+ as electron donor, analogous to Barge et al. reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: The aldehyde group of glutamic semialdehyde condenses with NH3 to form an imine, which is then reduced by Fe2+ (or other prebiotic reductants) to give the primary amine, yielding ornithine (2,5-diaminopentanoic acid).
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of aldehydes by NH3 is among the most well-established prebiotic reactions (Barge et al., PNAS, 2019). This specific transformation (glutamic semialdehyde → ornithine) mirrors the biological pathway's net outcome but achieves it in two simple chemical steps (rxn_014 + rxn_015) rather than the 8 enzymatic steps of modern biosynthesis (Cunin et al., Microbiological Reviews, 1986). The intermediate glutamic semialdehyde spontaneously cyclizes to Δ1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate in solution, which is a proline precursor — consistent with Patel et al.'s observation that arginine and proline precursors form together.

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide on montmorillonite

conditions.other: pH 8-10, wet-dry cycling may enhance yield by concentrating reactants, montmorillonite selectively adsorbs cationic products

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of ornithine's delta-amino group to the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide: H2N-(CH2)3-CH(NH2)-COOH + H2N-C≡N → arginine. The delta-amino group is more nucleophilic than the alpha-amino group (which is partially deactivated by the adjacent carboxyl), providing regioselectivity. Montmorillonite concentrates ornithine and preferentially retains the cationic arginine product via cation exchange.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction was first demonstrated by Schulze & Winterstein (Berichte der Deutschen Chemischen Gesellschaft, 32:3191, 1899) using Ba(OH)2. Tsubokura et al. (Synlett, 25:1302-1306, 2014) achieved up to 95% yield in water at 100°C with Sc(OTf)3 catalyst. Montmorillonite is proposed as the prebiotic catalyst because: (1) it selectively adsorbs cationic amino acids like arginine with Langmuir constants 30-50× greater than neutral amino acids (Catalano et al., Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, 2024); (2) it is a ubiquitous early Earth mineral; and (3) its Lewis acid interlayer cations can activate the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide analogously to Sc3+.

reactions.name: Ornithine formation in volcanic spark discharge

conditions.catalyst: volcanic ash and sulfide minerals as catalytic surfaces
conditions.other: Electric discharge through volcanic gas mixture (CO2, H2, NH3, H2S, H2O), collected in liquid water

reactions.mechanism: Electric discharge through volcanic gas mixtures generates reactive intermediates (HCN, aldehydes, NH3, aminonitriles). Ornithine likely forms via Strecker synthesis on an appropriate C4 amino aldehyde intermediate produced in the discharge plasma, followed by hydrolysis in the liquid water trap. The specific mechanism was not fully elucidated by Johnson et al.
reactions.reasoning: Johnson et al. (Science, 322:404, 2008) detected ornithine in reanalysis of Miller's volcanic spark discharge apparatus using modern HPLC-FD and ToF-MS with sub-picomolar sensitivity. Parker et al. (PNAS, 108:5526-5531, 2011) confirmed diamino acids in the H2S spark experiment. Ornithine was also tentatively identified in the Murchison meteorite (Meierhenrich et al., PNAS, 101:9182-9186, 2004). This is the most directly experimentally supported route to ornithine.

reactions.name: Trace arginine formation under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions

conditions.other: Supercritical CO2 + liquid H2O biphasic system, acid hydrolysis required to detect product

reactions.mechanism: Mechanism not fully elucidated. Hydroxylamine and pyruvate react under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions through unknown intermediates. Proposed: condensation, oligomerization, and rearrangement steps facilitated by the unique solvation properties of supercritical CO2. Only trace arginine detected (0.32 μmol vs 60.7 μmol alanine).
reactions.reasoning: Fujioka et al. (International Journal of Molecular Sciences, 10:2722-2732, 2009) detected 0.32 μmol arginine from hydroxylamine hydrochloride + pyruvic acid under supercritical CO2/H2O (60°C, >73 atm) after acid hydrolysis. This is the only direct experimental detection of abiotic arginine synthesis, though at trace levels and with unexplained mechanism. The starting materials (hydroxylamine, pyruvate) are themselves prebiotic intermediates. Included for completeness but rated as the weakest pathway.

reactions.name: Hydroxylamine formation from nitrite reduction

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 photocatalysis or Fe2+ reduction of NOx
conditions.other: UV irradiation, surface pools with dissolved Fe2+ and nitrogen oxides from lightning

reactions.mechanism: NH3 is partially oxidized by UV/photocatalysis to NH2OH (hydroxylamine), or alternatively, nitrogen oxides (NOx) from lightning are reduced by Fe2+ or H2 to hydroxylamine. Hydroxylamine is an intermediate oxidation state of nitrogen between NH3 and NO.
reactions.reasoning: Hydroxylamine is a recognized prebiotic nitrogen intermediate. It forms from partial oxidation of ammonia or partial reduction of nitrite. Summer & Karl (Science, 308:521, 2005) discussed nitrogen redox cycling on early Earth. Kasting (Science, 259:920, 1993) described lightning-generated NOx reduction pathways. Hydroxylamine is required as a starting material for the Fujioka et al. (2009) supercritical route.

pathways.name: Full cyanosulfidic pathway (Patel/Sutherland route)
pathways.description: The primary literature-supported route from simple molecules to arginine via the cyanosulfidic protometabolism network. HCN and acetylene are cross-coupled to acrylonitrile, which is aminated and reduced to beta-aminopropionaldehyde, guanylated by cyanamide, then converted to arginine via Strecker chemistry and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Spark discharge ornithine + cyanamide guanylation
pathways.description: Ornithine produced directly in volcanic spark discharge is guanylated by photochemically derived cyanamide on montmorillonite clay surfaces. The simplest conceptual route to arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal autotrophic route via rTCA and glutamate
pathways.description: Full autotrophic synthesis starting from CO2 and H2 at hydrothermal vents. CO2 is fixed to pyruvate, extended to alpha-ketoglutarate via rTCA steps on Fe0, reductively aminated to glutamate, reduced to glutamic semialdehyde, aminated to ornithine, and guanylated by cyanamide to arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN to cyanosulfidic arginine
pathways.description: HCN is generated hydrothermally from CO2 via formate and formamide, then transported to surface pools where it enters the cyanosulfidic pathway to arginine. Demonstrates Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical environmental flow.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN-derived cyanamide + spark ornithine
pathways.description: HCN from hydrothermal formate/formamide dehydration is photochemically converted to cyanamide at the surface. Spark discharge produces ornithine. The two meet on montmorillonite to yield arginine.

pathways.name: Glutamate from hydrothermal reductive amination + surface guanylation
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate from hydrothermal rTCA chemistry is reductively aminated to glutamate, which is transported to surface/biochemical pools for reduction to glutamic semialdehyde, amination to ornithine, and guanylation with surface-derived cyanamide.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal-to-surface HCN cyanosulfidic with alternative cyanamide
pathways.description: Variant of P4 where cyanamide is generated independently from atmospheric HCN (not hydrothermal), while the cyanosulfidic backbone intermediates use hydrothermal-derived HCN. Two independent HCN pools converge.

pathways.name: Pyruvate-derived ornithine with cyanosulfidic cyanamide
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate is extended to alpha-ketoglutarate, reductively aminated to glutamate, converted to ornithine, and combined with cyanamide from the cyanosulfidic network (rather than simple UV photochemistry).

pathways.name: Supercritical CO2 trace arginine route (Fujioka)
pathways.description: Trace arginine formation from hydroxylamine and pyruvate under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions. Hydroxylamine from surface photochemistry, pyruvate from hydrothermal CO2 reduction. The weakest but only directly experimentally demonstrated route to abiotic arginine.

pathways.name: Combined cyanosulfidic beta-aminopropionaldehyde + hydrothermal glutamate convergence
pathways.description: Beta-aminopropionaldehyde from the cyanosulfidic network and glutamate from hydrothermal rTCA both contribute to ornithine formation. Glutamate provides the carbon backbone via glutamic semialdehyde while the cyanosulfidic network provides cyanamide for guanylation. This pathway demonstrates the maximum environmental interconnection.
